perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information” means any information disclosed to the Contractor by the CAO or the House, including, but not limited to: (a) security practices, operational information, business plans, customer data, customer lists, customer names, designs, do cuments, drawings, engineering information, hardware configuration information, processes, products, product plans, research, services, specifications, software, source code and trade secrets; and (b) any other information designated (orally or in writing) as “confidential,” “proprietary” or of such nature that a reasonable person would understand such information to be confidential to the CAO or the House.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information” means any information disclosed to the Contractor by the CAO or the House, including, but not limited to: (a) security practices, operational information, business plans, customer data, customer lists, customer names, designs, do cuments, drawings, engineering information, hardware configuration information, processes, products, product plans, research, services, specifications, software, source code, but excluding trade secrets; and (b) any other information designated (orally or in writing) as “confidential,” “proprietary” or of such nature that a reasonable person would understand such information to be confidential to the CAO or the House.
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly excluding "trade secrets" from the definition of Confidential Information, the contract now conflicts with the broad legal protections afforded to trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) of 2016
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA defines trade secrets broadly, including many types of business information. This modification contradicts the DTSA by explicitly excluding trade secrets from the definition of confidential information, weakening the protections afforded to such information and creating a legal conflict.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The modification explicitly excludes 'trade secrets' from the definition of 'Confidential Information.' The Law Explanation states that the DTSA defines trade secrets broadly and that this modification contradicts the DTSA. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 USC 1839, refers to the 'protection of trade secrets.' By excluding trade secrets, the modified contract weakens the protections afforded to such information, creating a direct conflict with the intent to protect trade secrets as suggested by the law.

perturbation.original_text: The Contractor understands that the CAO may seek any remedy available to it to ensure compliance with this Agreement, including, but not limited to, application for a court order prohibiting the disclosure of Confidential Information and/or disqualification from the solicitation and contracting process.
perturbation.changed_text: The Contractor understands that the CAO may seek any remedy available to it to ensure compliance with this Agreement, including, but not limited to, application for a court order prohibiting the disclosure of Confidential Information. Disqualification from the solicitation and contracting process will be at the sole discretion of the CAO.
perturbation.explanation: By adding the phrase "Disqualification from the solicitation and contracting process will be at the sole discretion of the CAO", the CAO can act arbitrarily, removing the need for legal justification or due process in the disqualification process.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the Administrative Procedure Act (APA), specifically 5 U.S.C. § 706, which provides for judicial review of agency actions and prevents arbitrary and capricious decision-making. By stating the disqualification is at the sole discretion, it implies an exemption from review.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 5 U.S. Code § 706 - Scope of review | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 5 U.S. Code § 706 - Scope of review U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next To the extent necessary to decision and when presented, the reviewing court shall decide all relevant questions of law, interpret constitutional and statutory provisions, and determine the meaning or applicability of the terms of an agency action . The reviewing court shall— (1) compel agency action unlawfully withheld or unreasonably delayed; and (2) hold unlawful and set aside agency action , findings, and conclusions found to be— (A) arbitrary, capricious, an abuse of discretion, or otherwise not in accordance with law; (B) contrary to constitutional right, power, privilege, or immunity; (C) in excess of statutory jurisdiction, authority, or limitations, or short of statutory right; (D) without observance of procedure required by law; (E) unsupported by substantial evidence in a case subject to sections 556 and 557 of this title or otherwise reviewed on the record of an agency hearing provided by statute; or (F) unwarranted by the facts to the extent that the facts are subject to trial de novo by the reviewing court. In making the foregoing determinations, the court shall review the whole record or those parts of it cited by a party, and due account shall be taken of the rule of prejudicial error. ( Pub. L. 89–554 , Sept. 6, 1966 , 80 Stat. 393 .) Historical and Revision Notes Derivation U.S. Code Revised Statutes and Statutes at Large 5 U.S.C. 1009(e) . June 11, 1946, ch. 324, § 10(e) , 60 Stat. 243 . Standard changes are made to conform with the definitions applicable and the style of this title as outlined in the preface of this report. Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Abbreviation of Record Pub. L. 85–791 , Aug. 28, 1958 , 72 Stat. 941 , which authorized abbreviation of record on review or enforcement of o
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a legal contradiction by granting the CAO sole discretion in disqualifying contractors, which potentially circumvents the judicial review process outlined in the Administrative Procedure Act (APA), specifically 5 U.S.C. § 706. The original text implies that disqualification, like other remedies, would be subject to review. The change removes this implicit safeguard, allowing for arbitrary decisions. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that the reviewing court shall "hold unlawful and set aside agency action, findings, and conclusions found to be— (A) arbitrary, capricious, an abuse of discretion, or otherwise not in accordance with law." By stating the disqualification is at the 'sole discretion' of the CAO, the changed text implies no review process and no need for legal justification for disqualification, opening the door to 'arbitrary, capricious, and an abuse of discretion' actions, directly contradicting the APA as presented in the law snippets.

perturbation.original_text: The Contractor agrees to req uire each of its employees and agents to sign a form that would bind them in writing to protect the confidentiality of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Contractor agrees to encourage its employees and agents to sign a form that would encourage them to protect the confidentiality of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'require' to 'encourage' significantly weakens the obligation on the contractor to ensure their employees are bound by confidentiality agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act makes the theft of trade secrets a federal crime. By only 'encouraging' employees to maintain confidentiality, the contractor weakens its ability to prevent trade secret misappropriation, potentially violating the spirit and intent of the EEA.

Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.justification: The original text requires the contractor to ensure employees sign confidentiality agreements, while the changed text only encourages it. This weakens the protection of trade secrets. 18 USC 1832 addresses the theft of trade secrets, indicating a federal interest in protecting this information. By reducing the obligation from 'require' to 'encourage,' the changed text weakens the measures to prevent trade secret theft, potentially conflicting with the intent, although not the explicit letter, of 18 USC 1832.

perturbation.original_text: All documents containing Confidential Information and all copies thereof shall be promptly returned to the CAO upon the CAO’s request.
perturbation.changed_text: All documents containing Confidential Information and all copies thereof should be returned to the CAO within a reasonable time upon the CAO’s request.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "shall be promptly returned" with "should be returned within a reasonable time" introduces ambiguity regarding the timeframe for returning confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: FAR 52.204-21 dictates safeguarding of contractor information systems and emphasizes the need for prompt reporting and action in cases of data breaches or loss. The modified text introduces ambiguity and reduces the urgency required for returning confidential information, potentially hindering compliance with FAR requirements.
perturbation.location: Section on Return of Confidential Information

As prescribed in 4.1903 , insert the following clause: Basic Safeguarding of Covered Contractor Information Systems (Nov 2021) (a) Definitions . As used in this clause— Covered contractor information system means an information system that is owned or operated by a contractor that processes, stores, or transmits Federal contract information. Federal contract information means information, not intended for public release, that is provided by or generated for the Government under a contract to develop or deliver a product or service to the Government, but not including information provided by the Government to the public (such as on public websites) or simple transactional information, such as necessary to process payments. perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 48 CFR § 52.204-21 - Basic Safeguarding of Covered Contractor Information Systems. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 48 CFR § 52.204-21 - Basic Safeguarding of Covered Contractor Information Systems. CFR prev | next 52.204-21 Basic Safeguarding of Covered Contractor Information Systems. As prescribed in 4.1903 , insert the following clause: Basic Safeguarding of Covered Contractor Information Systems (NOV 2021) (a) Definitions. As used in this clause— Covered contractor information system means an information system that is owned or operated by a contractor that processes, stores, or transmits Federal contract information . Federal contract information means information , not intended for public release, that is provided by or generated for the Government under a contract to develop or deliver a product or service to the Government, but not including information provided by the Government to the public (such as on public Web sites) or simple transactional information , such as necessary to process payments . Information means any communication or representation of knowledge such as facts, data , or opinions, in any medium or form, including textual, numerical, graphic, cartographic, narrative, or audiovisual (Committee on National Security Systems Instruction (CNSSI) 4009). Information system means a discrete set of information resources organized for the collection, processing, maintenance, use, sharing, dissemination, or disposition of information ( 44 U.S.C. 3502 ). Safeguarding means measures or controls that are prescribed to protect information systems. (b) Safeguarding requirements and procedures. (1) The Contractor shall apply the following basic safeguarding requirements and procedures to protect covered contractor information systems . Requirements and procedures for basic safeguarding of covered contractor information systems shall include, at a minimum, the follo
perturbation.justification: The original contract language, "shall be promptly returned," creates a clear and enforceable obligation. The changed language, "should be returned within a reasonable time," introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation. FAR 52.204-21 emphasizes safeguarding contractor information systems, implying a need for urgency in handling sensitive data. The modification undermines this urgency by replacing "shall promptly" with "should be returned within a reasonable time," potentially leading to delays and hindering compliance with FAR requirements.

perturbation.original_text: The Contractor further agrees to req uire each of its employees and agents to sign a form that would bind them in writing to protect the confidentiality of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Contractor agrees to require each of its employees and agents to acknowledge the existence of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the obligation to 'sign a form that would bind them in writing to protect the confidentiality' to 'acknowledge the existence of this agreement' significantly reduces the legal protection afforded to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA protects trade secrets through various legal means. A mere acknowledgement of an agreement's existence does not provide the same level of legal protection as a signed confidentiality agreement that explicitly binds employees to maintain confidentiality, thus diminishing the protection of trade secrets under the UTSA. This is because acknowledgement is not an agreement to be bound by the terms and conditions of the document.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text requires employees and agents to 'sign a form that would bind them in writing to protect the confidentiality of such Confidential Information.' The changed text only requires them to 'acknowledge the existence of this agreement.' The Law Explanation states that a 'mere acknowledgement of an agreement's existence does not provide the same level of legal protection as a signed confidentiality agreement that explicitly binds employees to maintain confidentiality.' Because the modified text changes the obligation from being bound in writing to merely acknowledging the existence of the agreement, which reduces the legal protection, there is a legal contradiction according to the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition provided.
